The Story of Cowboy Crude

Howdy! I heard Mr Crude from the Middle East gave you folks a version of his story last month. Now it's my turn.

I'm called WTI, aka West Texas Intermediate. I'm light and sweet, unlike Mr Crude that is medium and sour. 

My close relative is Forties from the North Sea - that guy is also known as Brent simply because he typically sets the price of Dated Brent. His cousin, the REAL Brent Crude gets quite annoyed as this confusion!

So Brent and I have a love-hate relationship. We are physical oil and we are also paper benchmarks. That's hell of a lot confusing for many people. But what matters is I am super powerful in the market nowadays, to the point that I could even set the price of Dated Brent! (Take that, cousin Forties!)

Anyway the world is in trouble now. Mr Crude is still stuck, not much moving out from the Strait of Hormuz. I'm happily being exported out of the USGC at a rate of about 5 mbd, depending on who's the best bidder. Korea and Japan are doing a pretty good job at securing my lot to make up for whatever they are missing. 

But I'm hearing it's still not enough. Who cares? I'll just gallop my way and keep on winning. Based on Sparta's Arbs Radar, it looks like I'm still the cheapest barrel around in town.

Yours truly,
Cowboy Crude 

P/S: Just a friendly reminder I don't make much diesel and fuel oil like Mr Crude does.

CHART OF THE DAY: The hottest commodity? 

It isn't oil or fertiliser. The commodity in question is weathering an even greater supply shock and record prices: The anchovy — and its derivative, fishmeal (key for global aquaculture).

My @Opinion column: https://t.co/f7q7NRui58 https://t.co/zwcqxXMaPC

🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE.

Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may be inflating exports a bit (from inventories). But whatever that contribution is, clearly June oil exports have surged to >95% of pre-war levels (and some tanker trackers have UAE June oil exports even above pre-war levels).

With exports surging, the recovery in oil wellhead production won't be far behind, me thinks.

Importantly, UAE oil is flowing well beyond its typical trade partners. With Chinese demand down, Abu Dhabi is shipping lots of crude into Europe and the US.

In the first 4 months of 2026, US total oil production (crude, condensates and NGLs) has averaged 21.33m b/d — that's >900,000 b/d higher than in same period of 2025. And that's before the US shale industry had the opportunity to react to (and hedge) ~$100 a barrel.

COLUMN: The hottest commodity? 

One that's suffering from a major supply shock. It may sound like I’m talking about oil, but this is even more severe. Output is down 40% y-on-y and prices are up 80%.

The commodity in question? The anchovy.

U.S. Oil Recovery Threatened by Lower Prices

U.S. oil production accelerated to a record rate after the war with Iran and closure of the Strait of Hormuz boosted prices and dispelled some of the gloom hanging over the industry at the end of 2025.

Total production of crude and condensates accelerated to 13.9 million barrels per day in April, surpassing the previous record set six months earlier in October 2025.

In the short term, faster completions, unchoking existing wells and more production from stripper wells have all probably boosted output, but a more sustained increase requires more drilling ...

GEOPOLITICS, MACROECONOMICS & ENERGY
- US-Iran Diplomacy: President Trump weighed a return to all-out war but opted to stick with diplomatic talks following discussions with defense officials. US negotiators report positive technical discussions in Qatar. 
- Oil Glut Warning: Brent crude extended its decline below $72/bbl. Goldman Sachs warns the crude market will swing back into a massive oversupply, projecting a surplus of over 3 million bpd next year. API reported a 6 million barrel crude draw ahead of today's DOE data.
- USMCA Sunset: The Trump administration is expected to formally declare on Wednesday that it will not extend the USMCA trade agreement, triggering a "sunset clause" that starts a decade-long clock to wind down the North American free trade zone.
- Historic Yen Weakness: USD/JPY hit a fresh 40-year high of 162.82 with no immediate signs of BOJ intervention. Analysts note there are no major technical chart levels until the Plaza Accord era of 240.00 yen.
- UK Political Transition: The British pound suffered its weakest start to a year since 2022. Investors are on edge as Labour Prime Minister Keir Starmer steps down, shifting focus to how likely replacement Andy Burnham will manage the UK economy and stretched finances.

FEDERAL RESERVE & CENTRAL BANKS
- Sintra Symposium: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh speaks on a highly anticipated panel at 9:00 a.m. ET at the ECB's Sintra conference, alongside Lagarde, Bailey, and Macklem. Traders are scrutinizing Warsh for rate path clues after his strict price-stability pledge at the June FOMC.
- Rate Hike Odds: Futures markets currently price a 33% chance of a Fed rate hike later this month, and a 67% to 88% probability of a hike by September.
- ECB Outlook: Eurozone inflation eased significantly last month, drastically reducing pressure on the European Central Bank. Markets currently price just a 5% chance of an ECB rate hike on July 17.

EQUITIES, TECH & CORPORATE M&A
- Q2 Tech Performance & NVDA: The S&P 500 (+15%) and Nasdaq (+21%) just posted their best quarters since 2020. However, under the hood of the record-breaking SOX index, Nvidia (NVDA) was stunningly the worst single-performing stock in the benchmark for the quarter.
- Mega-M&A Record: Driven by $10B+ mega-deals, global M&A hit a record $2.8 trillion in the first half of 2026 (up 48% YoY), despite the total number of announced deals falling to a six-year low.
- Earnings & AI Growth: Consensus expects S&P 500 EPS to grow 22% YoY in the upcoming earnings season. AI infrastructure companies are projected to drive 60% of that growth, expanding earnings at triple the rate of the broader index.
- AI Job Cuts: The financial and information sectors are seeing accelerating job losses (averaging 28,000/month in 2026) as tech companies increasingly cite their heavy AI investments as the catalyst for workforce reductions.
- South Korea Pension Fears: NPS Chairman Kim Sung-joo dismissed fears of a 74 trillion won "selling bomb" in domestic equities, stating the chance of disruptive selling is "zero" even as a temporary waiver on domestic equity rebalancing expires.

DOMESTIC POLITICS
- Progressive Upsets: Democratic socialists scored major primary upsets in Colorado, including Melat Kiros unseating 29-year incumbent Diana DeGette. Combined with recent wins in NY and Maine, progressive momentum is disrupting the establishment status quo.
- Student Loan Rollout: New Department of Education repayment plans, implemented under the Trump administration, go into effect on July 1. Featuring stricter borrowing caps, the changes are expected to trigger "massive sticker shock" for millions of borrowers transitioning to new plans this summer.

text:
据高盛判断，全球石油接下来会重新转向供应过剩，两个原因：1、伊朗战争对油市的冲击正在消退。2、霍尔木兹海峡的航运流量也在恢复。 高盛这个判断，正确概率多大？我的判断65%~70%。

第一，“战争溢价消退”，正确概率很高，大约 75%—85%，这个已经在价格和航运数据里发生了，路透调查显示，31位经济学家/分析师，已经把2026年布油均价预期从上月的 90.44美元 下调到 84.50美元，原因就是霍尔木兹海峡重开、供应中断担忧缓解。路透还提到，布油已经从此前 126美元以上 的高点大幅回落。

第二，“油市重新转向供应过剩”，方向应该是成立的，但时间点没那么稳，概率大约 60%~70%。

核心证据就是，需求明显被高油价和供应扰动打伤了，IEA 6月报告把2026年全球石油，需求预测下调为同比下降110万桶/日，同时预计2027年供应，会暴增约 800万桶/日，而需求只增加约 200万桶/日，这就是典型的过剩结构。

 EIA也预计2026年全球石油需求将下降 110万桶/日，相比此前“增长”的预测明显转空。 Rystad 更直接，认为全球原油市场可能在7月重新进入过剩，并预测2027年可能出现 500万桶/日 级别的过剩。

不过，“马上过剩”这个说法，我会打折，概率只有 50%~55%。

因为霍尔木兹不是开了就等于完全正常。路透航运跟踪显示，虽然油轮正在重新进入海湾，船东和租船方也在提前布局，但实际装载出口流量仍然大约只有危机前的一半，安全、保险、航道清理和拥堵问题都还没完全解决。 IEA也提醒，虽然美伊临时协议为中东出口恢复铺路，但排雷、通行安排和政治限制仍然会拖慢完全恢复。

所以我的结论是，高盛这次“方向判断”大概率是对的，但“节奏判断”存在风险。

油市最可能的路径是，先去战争溢价 → 油价继续回落或震荡下行 → 库存从极低位开始修复 → 到2026年四季度或2027年更明显转向过剩。

但短期内不能忽视一个问题，库存被抽得太狠了。 IEA称，战争以来全球可观察库存平均以 380万桶/日 的速度下降，5月单月库存下降 1.43亿桶。这意味着即使供应恢复，市场也可能先经历一段“补库存需求”，不一定马上崩。

总结一下，我感觉伊朗战争溢价继续消退80%，霍尔木兹流量继续恢复，但不完全顺畅70%，2026年下半年油市从短缺转向小幅过剩60%，2027年出现明显供应过剩70%~75%油价马上单边暴跌不到50%

对交易来说，高盛这个观点偏利空油价、利空传统能源股，利好航空、化工、消费和通胀回落交易。但如果霍尔木兹恢复反复、保险费不降、OPEC+主动限产，油价可能不会按高盛设想那么顺地下去。

一句话,高盛看对“大方向”的概率偏高，大概三分之二；但短线别把它理解成“油价马上崩”，更像是战争溢价退潮后，市场重新回到供应过剩叙事。

Daily Market Wrap 02/07/2026

US equities closed modestly lower overnight as investors pared risk ahead of today's key US Non-Farm Payrolls report. Markets were also weighed by comments from Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who reiterated the Fed's commitment to returning inflation to its 2% target.

Technology stocks led the weakness, dragging the Nasdaq down 0.66%, while the S&P 500 slipped 0.22%. The Dow was broadly flat, easing just 0.03%.

US Treasury yields moved higher ahead of the employment data, with the 10-year yield rising to 4.48%, while the US dollar strengthened, lifting the DXY to 101.40.

Oil prices fell sharply as easing concerns around potential disruption to Strait of Hormuz shipping routes following US-Iran talks reduced geopolitical risk premiums. Brent crude dropped 2.6% to US$71.08/bbl and WTI fell 1.3% to US$68.58/bbl.

Gold traded choppily but ended higher, gaining 0.6% to US$4,029.89/oz as ongoing geopolitical uncertainty offset headwinds from a firmer US dollar.

Focus today: Markets are squarely focused on the US jobs report, with Non-Farm Payrolls, Average Hourly Earnings, the Unemployment Rate and Weekly Jobless Claims expected to shape expectations for the Fed's policy path and drive near-term market direction.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 2, 2026

Market Brief

Diplomatic Minister Wang Yi spoke with US Secretary of State Rubio

Chinese automakers released June sales figures: BYD led the pack, Leapmotor hit a new monthly high

A-share trading rules to take effect on July 6

US-Iran indirect talks held in Doha; Iran accuses US of violating memorandum commitments

Fed Chair Walsh: Inflation risks have eased, reaffirms no forward guidance on interest rates

Global equities diverged Wednesday: A-share market styles shifted again; “internet rumors” trigger sell-off in South Korea

Semiconductor price surge spreads to advanced packaging; Advantest raises quotes by over 20%

Meta plans to enter cloud market by selling excess AI compute capacity; shares surge

Top Stories

The United States and Iran held indirect technical talks in Doha, Qatar, with Qatar and Pakistan acting as mediators. The discussions focused on implementing the US-Iran memorandum of understanding, including the release of frozen Iranian funds and ensuring maritime security in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran accused the United States of violating commitments under the memorandum and plans to establish a monitoring mechanism to review violations. US Vice President Vance said the indirect talks progressed smoothly and that nuclear-related discussions would begin soon. Qatar’s Foreign Minister Mohammed stated that all parties agreed to continue talks over the coming period and schedule the next round shortly after the funeral of the former Iranian Supreme Leader.

Fed Chair Walsh reiterated at the European Central Bank’s central banking forum that the Fed would not provide forward guidance on future interest rate policy. Walsh said inflation risks have declined but levels remain too high. The Fed will “pave a new policy path” and hopes for a full discussion at the July meeting. He emphasized the Fed’s policy independence and continued balance sheet reduction. Walsh also noted that AI is reshaping the economy, but whether it has an inflationary effect remains to be seen.

After more than two months of technical and operational preparation, new A-share trading rules will take effect on Monday, July 6. The reforms optimize fund closing trading mechanisms, expand the range of securities eligible for post-market fixed-price trading, and increase the daily price limit for ST stocks on the main board to 10%.

AI-driven demand for semiconductors is fueling a new round of price increases in advanced packaging. Global leading outsourced semiconductor assembly and test provider ASE has revised its packaging quotes again, with increases of over 20% in some cases. The price hike covers various advanced packaging technologies, including wafer-level chip-scale packaging and fan-out wafer-level packaging. Market participants expect other packaging and test firms to follow suit.

China Macro & Policy

Diplomatic Minister Wang Yi spoke with US Secretary of State Rubio. Wang emphasized that building a constructive and stable strategic relationship aligns with the aspirations of both peoples and the international community, and serves the fundamental interests of both nations. Both sides agreed the call was positive and constructive and committed to implementing important consensus reached by their leaders, maintaining communication through flexible channels.

China’s Ministry of Commerce released data showing that from January to May, China’s total service trade reached 3.099 trillion yuan, up 6% year-over-year. Exports amounted to 1.23 trillion yuan, up 15.9%, while imports totaled 1.869 trillion yuan, up 0.4%. The service trade deficit narrowed to 638.56 billion yuan, a reduction of 160.72 billion yuan year-over-year.

China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology reported that from January to May, the value-added output of China’s electronic information manufacturing sector rose 14.6% year-over-year, 9.2 percentage points higher than overall industrial growth and 1.5 percentage points higher than high-tech manufacturing growth. In May, the sector’s value-added output grew 17% year-over-year.

China Equities Markets

A-share markets showed a divergence between large and small caps. The SSE Index rose 0.44% to 4,112.45, while the Shenzhen Component Index fell 0.53%, the ChiNext Index dropped 1.89%, and the Star 50 Index slid 2.48%. The Wind All China Index gained 0.19%. Market turnover reached 3.68 trillion yuan. Financial services led gains, with securities and insurance stocks outperforming. Livestock, power, electronic gases, fluorochemicals, and innovative pharmaceuticals rose. Energy storage declined, led by inverters. PCB and computing chip sectors weakened.

Hong Kong markets were closed on July 1 due to the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region’s anniversary holiday. The Taiwan Weighted Index closed up 1.94% at 47,018.99, a record closing high. TSMC rose nearly 4%, MediaTek and Delta Electronics gained over 2%.

In the first half of 2026, China’s securities regulator continued its strict oversight: 59 companies, including Juliy Suspenders and Huanri Century, and 13 executives or shareholders, including Chen Ayu of ST Xilinmen, were investigated by the China Securities Regulatory Commission—an increase of about 44% and 117% year-over-year, respectively. Disclosure violations remained the primary focus, with several firms fined millions of yuan for “capitalizing on trends.”

As of June 30, over 700 listed companies on the A-share market executed share repurchases this year, totaling more than 66 billion yuan—the highest level for the same period in history. Leading firms with strong cash flow and stable long-term profitability, particularly in liquor and home appliances, dominated repurchase activity.

Over 500 companies on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges issued risk warnings or abnormal price alerts in June, concentrated in semiconductors, electronic materials, and robotics—sectors with significant recent gains. Analysts warned that heavy concentration in single sectors often leads to high valuations and volatility, urging investors to avoid chasing trends, speculative hype, and potential short-term corrections.

Recent volatility in the AI sector has raised concerns about the sustainability of the rally. Reviewing mid-year 2026 outlooks from brokerages and asset managers, multiple institutions expect AI to remain the primary investment theme in the second half of the year, with further sectoral differentiation. Market sentiment is shifting from speculation toward fundamentals.

South Korean investors continued to increase positions in A-shares in the first half of 2026. Data from Korea Securities Depository showed South Korean investors actively allocated to Chinese assets via ETFs, particularly in technology, AI, semiconductors, and new energy themes. Sany Heavy Industry, Dongshan Precision, and Contemporary Amperex Technology ranked as the top three individual holdings.

The Securities Association of China released its fourth batch of IPO on-site inspection targets this year, selecting 21 companies. Eleven aim for the STAR Market, including “brain-computer interface first stock” Boincare and Hao Da Electronics. Yike Gas and Nade Power are targeting the ChiNext; Shenghua Bo and Kangbaijia are pursuing the main board.

China Industry News

Domestic automakers released June sales figures. BYD delivered 403,500 vehicles, up 5.5% year-over-year, retaining the top spot. Leapmotor delivered 93,376 new vehicles globally, a new monthly record and 95% year-over-year growth. Harmony Intelligent Mobility delivered over 50,000 units. NIO and XPeng each delivered over 40,000 vehicles. Zeekr, Deepal, Li Auto, and Xiaomi all delivered in the 30,000-unit range.

Domestic aviation fuel surcharges will be significantly reduced. Effective July 5, flights over 800 kilometers will charge 100 yuan per passenger, and flights 800 kilometers or less will charge 50 yuan—down 50 yuan and 30 yuan respectively. Multiple platforms reported that early summer travel ticket prices were nearly 20% lower year-over-year, with some routes cheaper than high-speed rail fares.

According to Centaline Property Research, June saw continued structural price increases in new residential properties across 100 major Chinese cities, with an average price of 17,184 yuan per square meter, up 0.16% month-over-month and 2% year-over-year. The average price of second-hand homes was 12,639 yuan per square meter, down 0.42% month-over-month, with only 12 cities recording price increases.

Beijing’s Housing and Urban-Rural Development Commission reported that second-hand home transactions in June reached 16,618 units, up 9.8% year-over-year—the highest level in five years for the same period. In the first half of 2026, Beijing’s second-hand home transactions totaled 935,830 units, up 5.7% year-over-year—the highest in five years for the same period.

According to Shenzhen Zhongyuan Research Center data, total residential transactions in Shenzhen in June reached 8,878 units, down 11.9% month-over-month but up 14.2% year-over-year—the highest June volume since 2021. Both new home presales and second-hand home volumes set six-year highs for the month.

Following price hikes by MLCC leader Yageo, multiple MLCC manufacturers at the Munich Shanghai Electronics Exhibition indicated they are preparing for price increases in the second half of the year. Industry leaders anticipate MLCC price increases will persist until the end of 2027, with high-capacity and ultra-high-capacity MLCC prices potentially rising through 2028–2029.

Fintech

Due to renewed disputes over platform developer protection clauses and the missed June 30 legislative window, the probability of the US Digital Asset Market Clarity Act passing in 2026 has sharply declined. The House Financial Services Committee plans to hold a dedicated hearing on July 17, which remains the fastest legislative path forward.

Taiwan passed its first dedicated cryptocurrency law, the Virtual Asset Services Act, shifting regulation from a registration system to a prior-approval regime. Existing virtual asset operators must submit license applications within 12 months, with a maximum review period of 21 months.

Corporate News & Updates

Sources revealed that Meta is developing a new cloud initiative to sell excess AI computing capacity and model access to external customers, positioning it to compete directly with Amazon AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud. Following the news, Meta’s shares rose 8.81%.

Anthropic announced the US Department of Commerce has lifted export restrictions on its most powerful models, Fable and Mythos, which resumed access on July 1. A global tech-sector controversy lasting less than three weeks has concluded.

Apple plans to purchase memory chips from ChangXin Memory Technologies and Yangtze Memory Technologies for devices sold in China. Apple intends to launch a basic M7 chip in the first half of next year and plans to introduce new iPad Pro and MacBook Pro models in the first half of 2027.

Elon Musk denied reports by a prominent financial media outlet that SpaceX had shown prototypes of an AI handheld device to investors ahead of its IPO, calling the claims “completely false.”

South Korea’s Fair Trade Commission announced it will investigate Google for allegedly abusing its dominant position in the Android app market and recommend corrective measures and fines. A Swedish court ruled that Google favored its own price comparison service in search results and must pay 1.97 billion US dollars in compensation to the buy-now-pay-later platform Klarna.

Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are negotiating second-half delivery prices with upstream substrate suppliers, with both companies favoring price reductions or withdrawal of previously announced increases. Earlier this year, Samsung and SK Hynix raised semiconductor substrate delivery prices by an average of 3% to 4%.

SK Hynix has signed long-term supply agreements with customers without price caps—a stark contrast to competitors like Micron, which routinely include price ceilings. This makes SK Hynix the only memory manufacturer currently unconstrained by pricing limits.

Avatr Technology announced it has received an L3 autonomous driving testing permit, with real-road testing now fully underway in Chongqing. The company plans to launch a production vehicle with L3 functionality in the second half of 2026.

Global Macro & Policy

US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer stated the United States will not renew its trade agreement with Canada and Mexico but will instead conduct annual reviews of the accord. The USMCA took effect on July 1, 2020, and expires in 2036.

European Parliament Committee on International Trade Chair Lange said the EU-US trade agreement took effect on July 1, along with a related “safety net” mechanism. In response to recent US President Trump’s tariff threats, Lange said the EU possesses policy tools to manage related risks.

US Institute for Supply Management data showed June manufacturing PMI fell 0.7 points to 53.3, below expectations of 53.9 but still near a four-year high. Declining oil prices drove the prices paid index down 9.1 points to 73, the largest monthly drop since July 2022.

ADP data showed US private sector employment increased by 98,000 in June, below the 119,000 forecast and the lowest gain since March, but marked the 12th consecutive month of growth.

South Korea’s June export value reached $102.25 billion, surging 70.9% year-over-year—the first time monthly exports exceeded $100 billion. Soaring demand and prices for memory chips drove semiconductor exports to $44.82 billion, up 199.5% year-over-year. The Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy expects semiconductor prices to remain elevated through the second half of the year.

Eurozone June CPI inflation fell to 2.8% from 3.2%, below market expectations of 3%. Core services inflation also eased to 3.2%. However, ECB officials remain cautious, stating inflation has not yet been fully contained and supporting either another rate hike or a pause to assess.

Bank of England Governor Bailey said the bank currently has no conditions to consider rate cuts, even though oil prices have fallen back to near pre-attack levels following US and Israeli strikes on Iran.

A United Nations independent expert panel warned that the pace of AI development has outstripped scientific understanding and government policy responses, meaning it cannot be guaranteed the technology will not cause catastrophic harm. UN Secretary-General Guterres urged nations to act swiftly to strengthen AI governance.

Global Equity Markets

US equities closed slightly lower. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.03% to 52,305.24, the S&P 500 dropped 0.22% to 7,483.23, and the Nasdaq Composite slid 0.66% to 26,040.03. Caterpillar fell nearly 7%, Walmart dropped nearly 4%, leading Dow declines. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index rose 1.53%, with Meta up nearly 9% and Microsoft up over 3%. SpaceX fell nearly 8%. The Nasdaq Composite, led by chip stocks, came under pressure. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index plunged over 6%, with Micron and SanDisk down more than 10%.

European equities ended mixed. The German DAX rose 0.18% to 25,040.28; the French CAC 40 fell 0.79% to 8,337.29; the UK FTSE 100 declined 0.18% to 10,478.34. Eurozone June inflation easing to 2.8% strengthened ECB rate cut expectations, but Fed Chair Walsh’s hawkish remarks sparked concerns over capital outflows, creating divergent market sentiment.

The South Korean KOSPI opened up nearly 2% but then reversed sharply after rumors surfaced that the government planned to establish a new think tank to “share” excess profits from chip giants, pressuring AI stocks and dragging the index down nearly 4%. The government swiftly denied the rumors as baseless, and the KOSPI pared losses to close down 2.04% at 8,303.41; Samsung Electronics fell nearly 6%. The Nikkei 225 closed up 0.59% at 70,474.96.

The National Pension Service’s temporary exemption from rebalancing its domestic equity holdings expired at the end of June. Amid a sharp rise in Korean equity market capitalization, speculation grew that NPS might need to sell large volumes of stocks to rebalance. NPS explicitly denied plans for “massive selling,” calling the speculation of a 7.4 trillion won sell-off absurd.

According to Infomax data, foreign investors sold a record 148.3 trillion won in Korean equities in the first half of the year—the largest net outflow on record. Meanwhile, individual investors bought approximately 99.2 trillion won, and institutional investors bought 35 trillion won, with retail investors absorbing the bulk of foreign outflows, much of it leveraged.

Michael Burry, the hedge fund manager portrayed in “The Big Short,” published a Substack column Tuesday reiterating his bearish view on AI and semiconductor stocks. Burry announced new short positions in NVIDIA, Applied Materials, Tesla, and the iShares Semiconductor ETF.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market reversed course, with major government bond yields rising. Five- and seven-year yields increased by 2 basis points. Government bond futures declined collectively, with the 30-year contract down 0.34%. The People’s Bank of China conducted 100 billion yuan in seven-day reverse repo operations, resulting in a net withdrawal of 1.1625 trillion yuan. Interbank liquidity tightened significantly, with the DR001 weighted average rate rising over 5 basis points to above 1.41%.

According to industry insiders, the interbank bond market may introduce a tiered market maker management framework, though no timeline has been set. Market participants remain divided on whether such structural reform is necessary under current conditions.

Amid ongoing regulatory crackdowns on inflated credit ratings and a wave of city investment bond rating withdrawals, Zhongcheng International has suspended its rating system, filling a regulatory gap on temporary rating suspensions. Analysts believe this move could eliminate inflated ratings, hidden risks, and information asymmetry, restoring ratings to their core function of risk disclosure.

US Treasury yields rose across the curve. The two-year yield increased 0.62 basis points to 4.176%, the three-year yield rose 1.43 basis points to 4.190%, the five-year yield climbed 1.23 basis points to 4.237%, the 10-year yield rose 1.58 basis points to 4.479%, and the 30-year yield gained 1.80 basis points to 4.970%.

Commodities

US crude oil futures fell 2.03% to $68.09 per barrel; Brent crude dropped 2.41% to $71.19 per barrel. Reduced tensions in the Middle East, gradual restoration of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, progress in US-Iran talks, and Iran’s resumption of crude exports have eased supply concerns. US crude production and exports reached record highs, and multiple institutions forecast global oil supply surplus. Slowing global industrial recovery has constrained demand, collectively pressuring prices.

Precious metals closed mixed. COMEX gold futures rose 0.15% to $4,044.60 per ounce; COMEX silver futures fell 0.53% to $59.60 per ounce. Uncertainty over Fed policy weighed on precious metals, while Middle East geopolitical risks supported safe-haven demand, with markets awaiting key data to determine direction.

Most base metals on the LME declined. LME nickel rose 0.63% to $16,390 per ton; LME aluminum rose 0.23% to $3,092.50 per ton; LME copper fell 0.33% to $13,330.50 per ton; LME lead fell 0.45% to $1,866.50 per ton; LME tin fell 0.48% to $51,320 per ton; LME zinc fell 1.68% to $3,492 per ton.

The World Gold Council’s mid-year outlook stated that after volatility in the first half of the year, gold will enter a critical phase in the second half, with performance shaped by geopolitical risks, interest rate environments, and investor sentiment. Gold will continue to serve as a global macroeconomic barometer.

A US official said crude oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz have resumed at over 10 million barrels per day. Iranian officials said Iran is prepared to sell oil to all countries except Israel and aims to expand exports using a 60-day US sanctions waiver, with India identified as a key target market.

Guangzhou Futures Exchange announced that, effective from July 3 settlement, the daily price limit for platinum and palladium futures will be adjusted to 14%, and the margin requirement to 16%.

FX & Currency Markets

Onshore renminbi against the US dollar closed at 6.7935 at 16:30, down 83 basis points from the previous close. The offshore rate ended at 6.7880. The PBOC’s central parity rate was set at 6.8067, up 42 basis points from the prior day.

At New York close, the US Dollar Index rose 0.23% to 101.41. Most non-US currencies declined: EUR/USD fell 0.38% to 1.1378, GBP/USD rose 0.11% to 1.3276, AUD/USD fell 0.38% to 0.6894, USD/JPY rose 0.01% to 162.6000, USD/CAD rose 0.13% to 1.4216, USD/CHF rose 0.14% to 0.8095, and offshore RMB/USD fell 34 basis points to 6.7947.

Daily Commodities Wrap-Up: July 1, 2026

Grains

The agricultural pits carried intense, broad-based buying momentum into Wednesday’s session, capitalizing fully on the dramatic structural reset triggered by Tuesday’s landmark USDA reports. Corn futures extended their post-report short squeeze, surging more than 2% to cross back over the $4.22 threshold as the market continued to digest the severe 120-million-bushel deficit in June 1 quarterly stockpiles. 

Wheat futures kept pace, marching higher as the electronic desks aggressively priced in the government’s deeper-than-expected cuts to all-wheat and spring wheat planted footprint. Soybeans also enjoyed steady, demand-driven buying, shaking off earlier acreage weight to participate in the macro ag liftoff. High-resolution weather models turning hot and dry across the northern I-states corridor over the next two weeks provided a strong secondary fundamental spark, amplifying automated buying.

This accelerated timeline means any localized high-pressure heat dome will land on an ag room that is highly sensitive to yield adjustments following the surprise drain in old-crop stocks.

Livestock

The cattle complex experienced a day of choppy, defensive consolidation on Wednesday, giving back a portion of yesterday's post-report gains to light speculative profit-taking. Traders stepped back to monitor initial mid-week cash trade indicators after early-morning country packer bids of $252.00 to $255.00 live were passed on with authority by feedlot managers. 

The underlying structural framework remains incredibly firm; northern show lists are noticeably smaller, and wholesale beef prices have maintained a strong posture near the key $400 mark. The long-term supply squeeze underscored by a year-to-date slaughter pace trailing nearly 9% behind last year’s clip continues to provide feedlots with ultimate leverage during negotiations.

Energies & Biofuels

WTI crude oil prices moved with a highly volatile, two-sided tone on Wednesday, coming under light structural pressure to trade just below the $69.00 level. The energy patch continues to sort through shifting geopolitical dynamics out of Doha, where diplomatic teams are attempting to salvage the fragile maritime peace framework. 

Despite the mild dip in the flat price, physical energy indicators remain exceptionally tight; the industry digested an implicit draw of over 6 million barrels in commercial crude stockpiles via the latest inventory tracking, marking the 11th consecutive weekly contraction in total domestic crude volumes and keeping product crack spreads defended.

Softs, Metals & Macros

The broader commodity space logged mixed performance as markets opened the third quarter of the year. In the softs ring, Canadian canola futures managed a slight positive finish, pulling minor support from the robust double-digit expansion across the U.S. soy complex. In the metals quadrant, gold and silver found a quiet stabilization floor, recovering from their worst quarterly performance in years as institutional capital cautiously realigned macro portfolios ahead of highly anticipated central bank labor and productivity updates.

The Pit Realignment

The technical landscape has officially flipped. The combination of tighter than expected old crop grain stocks and shrinking acreage profiles has fundamentally raised the price floor under the row crops. Performance tracking from today's open is solidly in the green for your grain positions, proving that the record short positions held by the fund community are still feeling the squeeze.

The Pollination Threat: Do not let your followers underestimate the shift in weather risk today. With the USDA corn stocks surprise eliminating 120 million bushels of projected carryout cushion, the market has zero room for an agronomic error. If the forecasted mid-July heat stress materializes across the Western Corn Belt during this early silking window, it will act as an immediate rocket underneath the September board.

Cash Cattle Discipline: The minor profit-taking pullback on the cattle board today means absolutely nothing to your cash bottom line. The fact that northern yards effortlessly batted away early $255.00 packer bids proves where the physical leverage sits. Keep your country asking prices firm the packers are critically short on uncommitted cattle and must come to the table before the week wraps up.

Energy Stock Tightness: While crude oil prices drifted lower due to macro asset reallocations, an 11th consecutive weekly draw in commercial crude inventories tells you the physical underlying pipeline is incredibly lean. This inventory vacuum should provide an ongoing structural backstop for bio-refining processing lines into late summer.

Tomorrow's Thoughts

Entering Thursday's session, the primary focus for the grain desks centers on whether the weather models confirm an intensifying dry pattern across the central I-states corridor, providing the technical clearance needed to challenge overhead resistance targets at $4.28 on September corn. In the livestock rings, look for cash cattle negotiations to break open in earnest. Armed with an expanding wholesale beef margin and exceptionally thin show lists, feed yards are well-positioned to squeeze additional premiums out of packers who cannot afford an empty hook.

The corvette "Soobrazitelny" thwarted an attack on an oil tanker

On June 30, an incident occurred in the Fehmarn Belt strait off the German coast involving the Russian warship "Soobrazitelny" (hull number 531), Greenpeace activists, and European politicians during the passage of the Panamanian-flagged tanker Kira K.

During the tanker's passage, Greenpeace activists and several European politicians attempted to stage a protest in inflatable boats.

🇩🇪 The German Federal Police vessel Bayreuth also approached the scene and demanded that the protesters keep their distance.

Then, the Russian corvette "Soobrazitelny" approached from the east at high speed. According to reports from those involved, it radioed the activists to move away from the tanker and then forced the boats to retreat.

There were no clashes or injuries; the action failed (for obvious reasons).

A Greenpeace activist wrote:

"Today I experienced firsthand how tense the situation has become with the 'shadow fleet' in the Baltic Sea.

The Russian warship RF-531 was not escorting the tanker Kira K, but approached rapidly from the east to assist it.

We knew that ships of the Russian fleet operate in this area.

Hearing over the radio: 'This is Russian warship RF-531 speaking, keep away from the Kira K' – that was very telling." After the incident, the Kira K continued its journey through the Baltic Sea towards the Danish Straits.

The tanker is on the sanctions lists of the EU, the UK, Canada, Australia, Switzerland, New Zealand, and Ukraine. In February 2026, the ship's captain was also sanctioned.
